---
editor_options: 
  markdown: 
    wrap: 72
---

# Spatial Interaction Modelling {#spatial_interaction}

## Introduction

![](images/banner-spatial-interaction.png)

### Aims {.unnumbered}

The aims of this practical are to:

1.  Understand the aims and common applications of spatial interaction
    (gravity) models.
2.  Understand the design and calibration of spatial interaction models.
3.  Apply spatial interaction models to a contemporary sociological
    issue.

### Application {.unnumbered}

To that end, we'll develop spatial interaction models to predict
internal migration flows between local authorities in England and Wales.

### Data {.unnumbered}

All data for this practical have been sourced from the Office for
National Statistics (ONS) at the local authority level and are found in
[`data/ons/local-authority`] as follows:

-   ONS internal migration flows for local authorities in England and
    Wales (2021)
    [[Source](https://www.ons.gov.uk/peoplepopulationandcommunity/populationandmigration/populationestimates/datasets/internalmigrationinenglandandwales)]
-   ONS district geometries (2021)
    [[Source]((https://geoportal.statistics.gov.uk/datasets/ons%3E::local-authority-districts-december-2021-boundaries-gb-bfc/explore?location=54.734360%2C-2.584695%2C7))]
-   ONS gross domestic product (GDP) per head at current market prices
    [[Source](https://www.ons.gov.uk/datasets/gdp-by-local-authority/editions/time-series/versions/2)]
-   ONS population
    [[Source](https://www.ons.gov.uk/peoplepopulationandcommunity/populationandmigration/populationestimates/datasets/estimatesofthepopulationforenglandandwales)]

### Tools {.unnumbered}

[Feature To
Point](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/data-management/feature-to-point.html?tabs=dialog)
(Data Management Tools), [Generate Near
Table](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/analysis/generate-near-table.html?tabs=dialog)
(Analysis Tools), [Generalized Linear
Regression](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/geoanalytics-desktop/generalized-linear-regression.html?tabs=dialog)
(GeoAnalytics Desktop Tools), [Notebooks in ArcGIS
Pro](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/arcpy/get-started/pro-notebooks.html),
[Calculate Geometry
Attributes](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/data-management/calculate-geometry-attributes.html?tabs=dialog)
(Data Management Tools)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Practical

### Pre-processing

[1] Introduce key concepts i.e., Newton's Law of Universal Gravitation.
Spatial flows between an origin and a destination are modelled as
proportional to their gravitational force and inversely related to their
spatial separation.

[2] Load Local Authority districts
[`data/ons/local-authority/LAD_DEC_2021_GB_BFC.shp`]

[3] Return Centroids using [Feature To
Point](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/data-management/feature-to-point.html?tabs=dialog)

-   Discuss sensitivity to feature geometry and calculation method,
    which can be tested via [Calculate Geometry
    Attributes](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/data-management/calculate-geometry-attributes.html?tabs=dialog)
-   The Centroids produced by ArcGIS differ slightly from those included
    in the Attribute Table from OS

[4] [Generate Near
Table](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/analysis/generate-near-table.html?tabs=dialog):

-   Centroids as Input and Near Features
-   Uncheck "Find only closest feature"
-   Method = Planar (using a suitable projected CRS)
-   [!] Save to a geodatabase so the output is more easily editable

[5] Load Gross domestic product (`ons-la-gdp.csv`) and population
(`ons-authority-population`) csv's and use [Join
Field](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/data-management/join-field.html?tabs=dialog)
to add to Centroids data, linking `LAD21CD` and `la_code`

[6] Use [Join
Field](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/data-management/join-field.html?tabs=dialog)
again, this time using the Near Table as input:

-   matching origin information (`IN_FID`) to `FID`
-   Use Field Mapping to select attributes of interest and update names:
    -   orig_LAD21CD (unique ID)
    -   orig_LAD21NM (name)
    -   orig_gdp
    -   orig_pop
    -   orig_long (or BNG_E)
    -   orig_lat (or BNG_N)

[7] Use [Join
Field](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/data-management/join-field.html?tabs=dialog)
again, this time for destination attributes:

-   matching destination information (`NEAR_ID`) to `FID`
-   Use Field Mapping to select attributes of interest with a new
    prefix:
    -   dest_LAD21CD (unique ID)
    -   dest_LAD21NM (name)
    -   dest_gdp
    -   dest_pop
    -   dest_long (or BNG_E)
    -   dest_lat (or BNG_N)

[8] Use Calculate Field to a create origin-destination field in the near
table (Text) i.e., !orig_LAD21CD! + "-" + !dest_LAD21C!

[9] Load internal migration data (`ons-internal-migration.csv`) where
`flow` represents the number of individuals (modelled). Encourage
exploratory analysis e.g., histogram, min-max.

[10] Use [Join
Field](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/data-management/join-field.html?tabs=dialog)
for the final time, this time linking based on origin-destination field,
transferring `flow` (time required)

[11] Before we can use the data, worth doing some cleaning i.e., Select
by Attributes and remove rows where orig_pop, dest_pop, orig_gdp,
dest_gdp = 0 or `flow` is null. This results in (\<) \~80,000 rows

### Data exploration

[12] Visualise some flow data. We can't do this for all bilateral
connections without causing ArcGIS stress, so:

-   Select by Attributes i.e., `flow` \> 1000 (n=\~450)
-   [XY to
    Line](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/data-management/xy-to-line.html?tabs=dialog),
    using `orig` and `dest` coordinates, selecting the appropriate CRS
-   Change symbology to size by `flow`
-   Finally remove the dataset, as quite complex

[13] We now have all the data we need to begin modelling, including:

-   Origin ID (`orig_LAD21CD`)
-   Destination ID (`dest_LAD21CD`)
-   Flow (`flow`, \# people)
-   Origin (vi) population (`orig_pop`)
-   Destination (wj) population (`dest_pop`)
-   Origin GDP (`orig_gdp`)
-   Destination GDP (`dest_gdp`)
-   Distance (`NEAR_DIST`, meters)

[14] Exploratory plots - Create Chart - Scatter Plot

> Are there any correlations?

### Model description

We'll be using a classic multiplicative gravity model:

$$T_{ij} = k \frac{V_i^μ W_j^α}{d_{ij}^{\beta}}$$

The model elements are as follows:

- $T_{ij}$ is the predicted flow between origin $i$ and destination
$j$.

- $V_i$ is a vector of origin attributes which relate to the
*emissiveness* of all origins $i$, i.e., this could be origin population and wealth (e.g.,
`orig_pop`, `orig_gdp`)

- $W_j$ is a vector of destination
attributes which relate to the *attractivenss* of all destinations $j$ i.e., destination population and wealth (e.g., `dest_pop`, `dest_gdp`)

- $d_{ij}$ is a matrix of costs (distance) between origins and
destinations ($ij$) i.e., the distance between local authority centroids.

- $k, μ, α, \beta$ are model parameters that need
to be estimated.

As the masses of the origin ($V_i$) and destination ($W_j$) increase, the predicted flow ($T_{ij}$) increases. In contrast, as distance ($d_{ij}$) increases (although this could be economic cost, travel time, ...), predicted flows decrease. 

This can also also be written as follows, as described by @wilson1971, which is mathematically identical but removes the fraction, so is simpler to handle:

$$T_{ij} = kV_i^μ W_j^αd_{ij}^{-\beta}$$

The key parameters that need to be estimated ($k, μ, α, \beta$) control the importance of the
variables they are associated with i.e., as $\beta$ increases, the cost associated with distance increase, and predicted flows would decrease, independent of $V_i$ and $W_j$. 

At this stage we don't know how to select those parameters, so we can start with a basic set, where effects scale linearly i.e., for a 1 unit increase in population, we might expect a 1 unit
increase in flows of people from that origin.

Newtons gravity uses a power law of $\beta=-2$, so here are the starting
values:

$$k=1, μ=1, α=1, \beta=-2$$

For $V_i$ (origin "emissiveness"), we will use origin population
(`orig_pop`) and for $W_j$ (destination "attractiveness"), we will use
destination GDP per capita (`dest_gdp`) i.e., as the population of the origin increases and the GDP per capita of the destination increases, we would expect a greater flow of people.

For $d_{ij}$, we'll use distance in kilometers (`NEAR_DIST` / 1000) where the reverse effect is expected i.e., as distance between local authorities increases, we would expect a smaller flow of people.

### Unconstrained and Total-constrained models

[15] In the table containing all the relevant attributes, Calculate Field (`gm1` i.e., gravity model one), Float, using the following equation (distance in km)

pow(!orig_pop!, 1) \* pow(!dest_gdp!, 1) \* pow(!NEAR_DIST!, 1000, -2)

This produces an unconstrained output (unitless), as we have not used
the $k$ factor, which converts our model to a *total constrained* version
by scaling to match the total number of flows i.e., sum of all observed
flows divided by the sum of all of the other elements in the model.

[16] Using Explore Statistics, return the sum of `flow` and `gm1` , and then calculate a
new field incorporating the scale factor (`gm1_scale`):

- Data = 3698001.8812999986 (sum of internal migrations) 
- Model = 63224.55841802744 (sum of `gm1`)

!gm1! * (3698001.8812999986 / 63224.55841802744)

*Note*: we can't do this all via Add Field, as this is a row-wise
calculation

[17] Model evaluation

First, check the total of `gm1_scale` matches the total flow (it does!),
confirming our model is *total constrained*.

However, the output is pretty abysmal here (R2 = 0.07, Create Chart,
Scatter Plot), because we haven't tuned our parameters!

### Tuning parameters

If we log both sides of the equation above, we get the following:

$$\mathrm{ln}T_{ij}=k+μ\mathrm{ln}V_i+α\mathrm{ln}W_j−β\mathrm{ln}d_{ij}$$

This is basically a regression model! This format allows us to tune of parameters. 

> **TO DO** Explain why.

However, there are problems with using a log-linear approach, and
instead it is more common to use Poisson regression, which is better for skewed
distributions (like our flow data):

$$λ_{ij}=\mathrm{exp}(k+μ\mathrm{ln}V_i+α\mathrm{ln}W_j−β\mathrm{ln}d_{ij})$$
> **TO DO** Explain Poisson distribution and regression

[18] Visualise Statistics - Histogram - Flow

[19] First, log[^1] the relevant independent variables:

- Add Field 
- math.log(!orig_pop!)
- math.log(!dest_gdp!)
- math.log(!NEAR_DIST! / 1000) \# converting to km 

[^1]: Note that Python math.log() uses `e` as the default, which is consistent with R, where log() uses `exp(1)` as the [default](https://www.rdocumentation.org/packages/base/versions/3.6.2/topics/log). For those more familiar with Excel, log() defaults to base10, which is different! For consistency, this can either be updated to log(val, EXP(1)) or the LN() function can be used. 

[20] Poisson uses count data so our `flow` values are invalid. Instead, we need to convert these to integer (`flow_int`) using int(!flow!) in Add Field

[21] Run [Generalized Linear
Regression](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/geoanalytics-desktop/generalized-linear-regression.html?tabs=dialog)

*Note* we must use the GeoAnalytics Desktop Tools version, as this allows for table inputs. 

Inputs:

- Flow (integer, dependent) 
- Logged variables (independent) 
- Count (Poisson)

[22] Evaluate and interpret coefficients

> **TO DO** include screenshot or formatted table here

*Note* we will use the model coefficients as our parameter estimates. 

[23] Produce `gm2` (calibrated model) and evaluate performance using Create Chart, Scatter Plot

Equation: math.exp(-5.4022+(0.9985 * !orig_pop_log!)+(0.2862 * !dest_gdp_log!)-(abs(-1.2354) * !near_dist_log!))

Performance: R2 = 0.45

### Origin-constrained models

[24] Introduce theory of origin constrained models

[25] Implementation in ArcGIS

This is technically possible in ArcGIS but not easy, requiring us to
use [Encode Field](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/data-management/encode-field.html?tabs=dialog) to create dummy variables for `orig_LAD21CD`. 

This works when there are a small number of categories to encode, but Generalised Linear Regression (tool) struggles with the number of LA names in our dataset (\~300) with multicollinearity issues 

Even if this was not the case, it is very difficult to manually include the coefficients in a regression equation, as we did in [22]

Instead, load a Python notebook and use the [appendix code](#python_notebook) to perform
GLR and create an origin-constrained model (`gm3`), saving output to csv.

> *Note*  while I don't expect the students to write the code independently, I will introduce it in a piecemeal fashion, explaining what each line does.

[26] Load predictions csv produced in the previous step and assess performance (`flow_int` vs. `gm3`):

- R2 = 0.57 
- Check that sum of modelled flows for each origin matches measured flows

### Destination-contrained models

[27] Challenge the students to run a destination-constrained model, adapting the Python code to produce `gm4`.

### Doubly-constrained models

[28] Outline the process for a doubly-constrained model

## Extra

Our models are based on a simple set of predictors, but you could of course include a much wider range e.g., median earnings rather than GDP per capita, unemployment rate, housing affordability, or the share of working-age population. 

If applying this model to international migration, we might include a completely different set of predictors e.g., cultural similarity, as covered in [Practical 2](#spatial_weights), whether countries share a common language, or the degree of conflict within or between countries. 

### Testing contiguity {#contig .unnumbered}

At our scale of analysis, we could investigate whether **contiguity** plays a role i.e., are flows larger between neighbouring local authorities? 

[29] Create numeric ID for LA geometries using SequentialNumber() 

[30] Create Spatial Weights Matrix (Contiguity Edges Corners)

[31] Convert Spatial Weights Matrix to Table

[32] Join SWM table to LA geometries: 

- NUMERIC_ID (origin) to numeric_ID, storing `orig_LAD21CD`
- NID (destination) to numeric_ID, storing `dest_LAD21CD` 

[33] Create origin-destination field, combining `orig_LAD21CD` and `dest_LAD21CD`

[34] Create constant bordering field in the SWM table (`contig` = 1) 

[35] Join Field: NEAR_TABLE to SWM based on origin-destination, linking `contig`. This produces 1 (bordering) or "<Null>" (not bordering) 

[36] Calculate Field, update `contig` using: 0 if !contig! is None else !contig! 

[37] Add binary contiguity [`contig` = 0/1] to the model

> To what extent does adding local authority contiguity improve our model?

### Python Notebook for GLR Poisson {#python_notebook .unnumbered}

```         
import os
import arcpy
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import statsmodels.api as sm
import statsmodels.formula.api as smf

# Current ArcGIS Pro project
aprx = arcpy.mp.ArcGISProject("CURRENT")
# List maps
m = aprx.listMaps()[0]

# Access table
table = m.listTables("la_near_table_v2")[0]
table_path = table.dataSource
workspace = arcpy.Describe(ta

# List of fields
fields = [f.name for f in arcpy.ListFields(table_path)]

# Data to pd
rows = arcpy.da.SearchCursor(table_path, fields)
df = pd.DataFrame(list(rows), columns=fields)

# Incorrect formula
### model_formula = "flow_int ~ C(ORIG_LAD21CD) + orig_pop_log + dest_gdp_log + near_dist_log - 1" 

# Create formula and run glm 
model_formula = "flow_int ~ C(ORIG_LAD21CD) + dest_gdp_log + near_dist_log - 1"
model = smf.glm(formula=model_formula, data=df, family=sm.families.Poisson()).fit()

# Summary
# print(model.summary())

# Predictions
df["gm3"] = model.predict(exog=df)

# Sets path to output (workspace + file name)
#out_table = os.path.join(workspace, "la_near_table_v2_pred.csv")

# Manual export to csv
df.to_csv(r"./la_near_table_v2_pred.csv", index=False)
```

## Resources

-   Wilson, A.G. ([1971](https://doi.org/10.1068/a030001)). A family of
    spatial interaction models, and associated developments. Environment
    and Planning A, 3(1), pp.1-32.
-   Haynes, K.E., & Fotheringham, A.S.
    ([1985](https://researchrepository.wvu.edu/rri-web-book/16/)).
    Gravity and Spatial Interaction Models. Reprint. Edited by Grant Ian
    Thrall. WVU Research Repository, 2020.
-   Fotheringham, A.S. and M.E. O’Kelly
    ([1989](https://www.amazon.co.uk/Spatial-Interaction-Models-Formulations-Applications/dp/0792300211))
    Spatial Interaction Models: Formulations and Applications. London:
    Kluwer Academic.
-   Dennett, A. and Wilson, A. ([2013](https://doi.org/10.1068/a45398)).
    A multilevel spatial interaction modelling framework for estimating
    interregional migration in Europe. Environment and Planning A,
    45(6), pp.1491-1507.
-   Rowe, F., Lovelace, R. and Dennett, A.
    ([2024](https://doi.org/10.4337/9781802203233.00019)). Spatial
    interaction modelling: a manifesto. In A research agenda for spatial
    analysis (pp. 177-196). Edward Elgar Publishing.
-   Liao, M. and Oshan, T.M.
    ([2025](https://doi.org/10.1111/gean.70001)). A Data‐Driven Approach
    to Spatial Interaction Models of Migration: Integrating and Refining
    the Theories of Competing Destinations and Intervening
    Opportunities. Geographical Analysis, 57(3), pp.540-554.
